title: Doubting
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A brother wandered forth with me,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Beside a barren beach:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He harped on things beyond the sea,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And out of reach.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He hinted once of unknown skies,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And then I would not hark,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But turned away from steadfast eyes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Into the dark.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And said ן¿½an ancient faith is dead
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And wonder fills my mind:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I marvel how the blind have led
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So long the blind.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Behold this truth we only know
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That night is on the land!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And we a weary way must go
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To find Godן¿½s hand.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I wept ן¿½Our fathers told us, Lord,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That Thou wert kind and just,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But lo! our wailings fly abroad
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For broken trust.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½How many evil ones are here
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who mocking go about,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Because we are too faint with fear
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To wrestle Doubt!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Thy riddles are beyond the ken
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of creatures of the sod:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Remember that we are but men,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Thou art God!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½O, doting world, methinks your stay
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Is weaker than a reed!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Our Father turns His face away;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Tis dark indeed.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The evening woods lay huddled there,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All wrapped in silence strange:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A sudden wind and lo! the air
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was filled with change.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Your words are wild,ן¿½ my brother said,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½For Godן¿½s voice fills the breeze;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Go hide yourself, as Adam did,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Amongst the trees.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I pluck the shoes from off my feet,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But dare to look around;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Behold,ן¿½ he said, ן¿½my Lord I greet,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On holy ground!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And God spake through the wind to me
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Shake off that gloom of Fear,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You fainting soul who could not see
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That I was near.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Why vex me crying day and night?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You call on me to hark!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But when I bless your world with light,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who makes it dark?

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Is there a ravelled riddle left
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That you would have undone?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What other doubts are there to sift?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I answered ן¿½None.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½My son, look up, if you would see
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Promise on your way,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And turn a trustful face to me.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I whispered ן¿½Yea.ן¿½
meaning:
This evocative piece by Henry Kendall represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Henry Kendall's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
